coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based dual-emission proportional fluorescence sensor for ultra-sensitive visual detection of mercury ions in natural water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mao
Huang, Hefei
Wang, Lei
Sun, Menglei
Hou, Haoting
Yang, Xudong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have become a hot topic in fluorescence sensing and bioimaging due to their superior optical properties, high sensitivity, and high selectivity for specific ubstances. In this experiment, a novel carbon quantum dot/copper nanocluster dual fluorescence emission composite (CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs) based on silica coating was constructed by a composite of CQDs@SiO2 and DPA-CuNCs, which can detect Hg2+ in water by the change of fluorescence color under UV light. Its detection limit for Hg2+ in aqueous solution is as low as 1 μM. In addition, CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites successfully achieved the specific detection of Hg2+ in water samples from the South Lake of Changchun City. Compared with the traditional detection methods, this fluorescent composite has the advantages of convenience and long-term stability, and is more suitable for the rapid detection of mercury ions in the environment, which increases the practical application capability of fluorescent nanocomposites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hg2+ detection
Fluorescence enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites
Detection of Hg2+ by CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite
Detection of Hg2+ in actual water samples
Structural characterization of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites
Study on fluorescence properties of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites
Study on the sensitivity of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs fluorescent composites for Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-doped CDs and (b) particle size distribution of N-doped CDs.
(a) XRD patterns of DPA-CuNCs; (b) XPS spectra of copper in DPA-CUNCs.

UV–visible absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of (a) DPA-CuNCs and (c) CQDs@SiO2; Inset: DPA-CuNCs and CQDs@SiO2 optical photos under the sun and UV lamp (365 nm); Fluorescence emission spectra of (b) DPA-CuNCs and (d) CQDs@SiO2 at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Emission spectra of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites at different excitation wavelengths (350–385 nm); (b) Emission spectra of CQDs@SiO2, DPA-CuNCs, and CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites; Insets are optical photos of CQDs@SiO2, DPA-CuNCs, and CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites under sunlight and UV lamps (365 nm), respectively.

(a) fluorescence response bars and (b) corresponding fluorescence response spectra of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites in the presence of different metal ions (Ag+, Ca2+, Cr6+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn7+, Ni+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+).
The fluorescence response profiles of (a) CQDs@SiO2 (Ex=350 nm) and (b) DPA-CuNCs (Ex=350 nm) after the addition of different Hg2+ concentrations (0.06–80 μM).
Fluorescence response profiles of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs at different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.06–100 μM)(Ex = 350 nm).
Plot of I650/I450 fluorescence intensity ratio versus Hg2+ concentration (0–100 µM), inset: Linear plot of I650/I450 fluorescence intensity versus Hg2+ concentration (0–5 µM).
Photos of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanocomposites exposed to daylight (top) and UV light (365 nm) in the Hg2+ (0–80 μM) concentration range (bottom).
CIE chromaticity plot of the (x, y) coordinates of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanocomposites as a function of Hg2+.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites and detection of Hg2+.
Determination of added Hg2+ in actual water samples using CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based dual-emission proportional fluorescence sensor for ultra-sensitive visual detection of mercury ions in natural water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have become a hot topic in fluorescence sensing and bioimaging due to their superior optical properties, high sensitivity, and high selectivity for specific ubstances. In this experiment, a novel carbon quantum dot/copper nanocluster dual fluorescence emission composite (CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs) based on silica coating was constructed by a composite of CQDs@SiO2 and DPA-CuNCs, which can detect Hg2+ in water by the change of fluorescence color under UV light. Its detection limit for Hg2+ in aqueous solution is as low as 1 μM. In addition, CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites successfully achieved the specific detection of Hg2+ in water samples from the South Lake of Changchun City. Compared with the traditional detection methods, this fluorescent composite has the advantages of convenience and long-term stability, and is more suitable for the rapid detection of mercury ions in the environment, which increases the practical application capability of fluorescent nanocomposites.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A novel CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite has been successfully designed and prepared. The resulting CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites exhibit two fluorescence emission peaks of different wavelengths when excited at a wavelength of 350 nm, and have a high fluorescence quenching effect in the presence of Hg2+.The orange-red fluorescence of DPA-CuNCs can be quenched by Hg2+ during the detection process, while the blue emission of CQDs@SiO2 remains stable. The detection limit of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite can be as low as 1 μM, and the new dual emission fluorescence nanocomposite was successfully used to detect Hg2+ in water samples from Changchun South Lake. This new dual-emission fluorescent nanocomposite, successfully used for detecting Hg2+ in water samples from South Lake of Changchun, is of great value for detecting heavy metal ions in the water environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (Hg2+) is a highly harmful metal ion, widely existing in air, water, soil, and biota [1,2]. Due to its highly toxic and bio-concentrated effects, Hg2+ can cause many serious adverse effects on human health, such as kidney failure, brain damage, and various cognitive and motor disorders. Today, every country has a certain degree of soil heavy metal pollution. Under the current situation of rapid economic development, the control of heavy metal pollution is essential, and the monitoring technology of heavy metals has gradually become necessary technical research for the development of the industry. Therefore, it is significant to develop sensitive sensing materials that recognize and detect Hg2+ in biological and environmental systems. Previously reported methods for the detection of Hg2+ in the aqueous solution include atomic absorption spectrometry [3], nuclear fluorescence spectrometry [4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5], and fluorescence spectrometry[6] and electrochemical analysis [7]. More recently, liquid crystal-based sensor systems and resonant Rayleigh scattering sensors have also been used to detect Hg2+ [8–11]. However, the application of these methods is limited by the disadvantages of expensive instruments, large size, complex sample pretreatment process, low sensitivity, and selectivity. Therefore, it is necessary to explore a new Hg2+ determination method.

Optical nanosensors for the determination of Hg2+ have attracted more and more attention, and many related sensors have been constructed. Some excellent reviews on this topic have recently been published in specialized journals based on the importance of nanomaterials applications. Xu et al. [9] reviewed the development of nanomaterial-based mercury detection methods according to the types of nanomaterials and discussed the speciation analysis of mercury by nanomaterials. Bottasini et al.[12–14] addressed the applicability of nanotechnology-based sensors for the determination and analysis of Hg2+ in actual samples and the colorimetric and visual determination of Hg2+ based on gold-based nanomaterials optical sensors based on nanomaterials have been studied from different perspectives, but the design strategies of Hg2+ optical nanosensors have not been comprehensively summarized, especially the fluorescence and SERS detection strategies.

With the development of nanoscience and technology, new fluorescent materials with different morphology and size distribution have been designed and synthesized. Such materials include semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), precious-metal-based nanoclusters, upconversion nanoparticles (NPs), and silicon quantum dots [15–20]. CQDs, a new type of carbon nanomaterial, have unique properties due to functional groups on their surface, such as amino group, oxygen, and polymer chain. Due to their unique fluorescence and surface properties and excellent water dispersion, these materials have been widely used in the field of fluorescence sensing and biological imaging. Carbon is the fourth most abundant element in the universe. In recent years, more and more carbon-based nanomaterials have been synthesized, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerenes, nanodiamonds, carbon dots (CDs), etc. [21,22] Such materials have unique geometric structures, large specific surface area, and excellent electrical, optical, thermal, and magnetic properties, which play an essential role in the fields of optoelectronic materials, new energy, biomedicine, and so on [23–26]. The nanostructure of CDs is entirely different from that of conventional semiconductor quantum dots. Among them, the core of CDs is mainly graphitized SP2 carbon, and the outer shell is rich in groups such as -COOH, -NH2, and -OH [27–31]. A variety of carbon sources, including organic molecules, biomass, and other carbon-containing materials, can be used to prepare CDs [32]. At present, some CDS-based fluorescent probes have been used for metal ion detection: Gao et al.[33] summarized the optical and chemical properties related to metal ion sensing and introduced the application of CDs in metal ion detection in detail, as well as some insights into sensing mechanism: Devi et al.[34] gave a comprehensive overview of CDs-based fluorescent probes for the detection of heavy metals in water. However, there are few types of research on the detection of organic molecules, biomolecules, and enzymes. Although there is no unified theoretical explanation for the luminescence mechanism of CDs, excellent optical properties, good water solubility, and biocompatibility, easy synthesis and surface modification of CDs have made CDs a hot topic in the field of fluorescence sensing and biological imaging in recent years.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm, Milli-Q) was used in all experiments. Ethyl orthosilicate, Shanghai reagent factory, Copper(II) nitrate trihydrate, a Shanghai reagent factory, Ethylenediamine, Beijing chemical plant, Citric acid, Beijing chemical plant, Glycerin, Beijing chemical plant, D - penicillamine, Changchun three state pharmaceutical technology co., LTD., Ethyl alcohol, Tianjin fine chemical co., LTD., Mercury chloride, Jiangsu sent instrument co., LTD., Ammonia water, Qingdao Yufengda Refinement Co., LTD., Sodium chloride, Qingdao Yufengda Refinement Co., LTD., Sodium hydroxide, Qingdao Yufengda Refinement Co., LTD., Hydrochloric acid, Qingdao Yufengda Refinement Co., LTD.

Magnetic agitators, MS-IIIS, Shanghai Xiaocong Technology Co., LTD., numerical control ultrasonic cleaner, KQ-500 V, Zhejiang Nader Scientific Instruments Co., LTD., centrifuge, 5428, Eppendorf, analytical balance, AUY-120, Tsushima Company, Japan, UV lamp, ZF-7A, Shanghai Xuchang Industrial Equipment Co., LTD., UV–visible spectrophotometer, Uv-759 s, Shanghai Yidai Analytical Instrument Co., LTD., X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, AXIS Nova, Shenzhen Koshita Electronic Technology Co., LTD., transmission electron microscope, JEOL-1400FLASH, Hitachi, Japan, refrigerator, BL-200SM300L, Guangdong Libo Electromechanical Co., LTD., X-ray diffractometer, 7000 S/L, Shenzhen Keshida Electronic Technology Co., LTD., fluorescence spectrophotometer, F97Pro, Shanghai Precision Instrument Co., LTD., Fourier transform infrared spectrometer, iS20, Jiangsu Vanke Scientific and Educational Instruments Co., LTD., electronic tensile testing machine, CN61M-LDS-2A, Beijing Haifida Technology Co., LTD., Ultra-high resolution thermal field emission scanning electron microscope, CLARA, Shanghai Haoxia Scientific Instruments Co., LTD.

In this experiment, CQDs were synthesized by microwave method. first, 2.0 g of citric acid was dissolved in 10 ml deionized water of deionized water and stirred, then it was dissolved in 1 ml of ethylenediamine and stirred, and finally it was heated in microwave oven for 2 min 8.1 ml of CQDs, 1 ml of ethyl orthosilicate, dissolved in 2 ml of ammonia and then 9 ml of ethanol were added, then the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 6 h, and the obtained The silicon dioxide particles were separated from the suspension by centrifugation, and finally washed with deionized water and ethanol and dried in an oven (60 ℃) to obtain CQDs@SiO2 as a light yellow solid.

At room temperature and stirred by magnetic stirrers, Cu(NO3)2 (150 mM, 100 μL) was added to penicillamine (25 mM, 3 ml) solution drop by drop, and 1.9 ml deionized water was added. When the two reactive agents were mixed, the solution turned brown, but when stirred for 2 h, the solution turned milky white. At this time, aqueous copper nanoclusters were synthesized. After comparison, when the molar mass ratio of penicillamine and copper nitrate is 1:6, the fluorescence effect of the synthesized aqueous copper nanocluster is the best. Therefore, this experiment is carried out under the condition that the molar mass ratio of penicillamine and copper nitrate is 1:6. In order to remove the excess ligand, the product is centrifuged at 8500 RPM for 5 min to separate the emulsion precipitate. It was purified by repeated washing three times with distilled water and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emitting composites were obtained by adding CuNCs dropwise to the CQDs@SiO2 mixture under room temperature and stirring with a magnetic stirrer under UV lamp irradiation until the colour of the solution fluoresced purplish red.

First, a series of Hg2+ solutions with different concentrations (0.06–100 μM) were prepared for subsequent use, and then a specific volume of Hg2+ solution was directly added to the CQDs@SiO2 solution. A fluorescence spectrometer measured the intensity, and the excitation wavelength was set to 350 nm. The fluorescence spectra in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.06–100 μM) were obtained and recorded.

First, a series of Hg2+ solutions with different concentrations (0.06–100 μM) were prepared for subsequent use, and then a specific volume of Hg2+ solution was directly added to the CuNCs solution. Fluorescence intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrometer, and fluorescence spectrograms in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.06–100 μM) could be obtained. And record it.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ by CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a series of Hg2+ solutions with different concentrations (0.06–100 μM) were configured for subsequent use. Then a specific volume of Hg2+ solution was added directly to the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanocomposites, and the fluorescence intensity was tested by a fluorescence spectrometer to obtain fluorescence spectra in the presence of different Hg2+ (0.06–100 μM) concentrations. The colour changes of the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emitting composites were observed by irradiation under UV lamps (365 nm) and in daylight and photographed and recorded in time.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual-emission fluorescence composite for Hg2+ detection, some other common metal ions were detected by CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual-emission fluorescence composite under the same experimental conditions. Such as Ag+, Ca2+, Cr6+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mn7+, Mg2+, Na+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+. The same volume of different metal ion solutions was added to CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs fluorescent nano solutions, and the final concentration of the added metal ions was 100 μM. The fluorescence spectra of different metal ions at the ratio of I650/ I450 were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm on a fluorescence spectrometer. Finally, the pre-prepared 100 μM Hg2+ was added to CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nano solution, and fluorescence spectra were collected again.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water from our laboratory and Nanhu Lake in Changchun was collected as water samples and filtered with a 0.22 μM filter membrane without other treatment. The detection process of Hg2+ in natural water samples by CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite was simulated by adding different concentrations of Hg2+ with the method of spiked recovery, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. ( Scheme 1).

The morphology and size of CPD were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The results show that the samples have an almost spherical structure and exhibit good dispersion in aqueous solution ( Fig. 1(a)), with an average particle size of about 2.20 ± 0.35 nm (Fig. 1(b)).

We carried out an X-ray diffraction analysis of the copper nanocluster solids. We used the measured diffraction peak positions and intensities to judge their structures and crystalline forms. As shown in Fig. 2(a), there is a broad peak for DPA-CuNCs at 2θ = 19° and 36° but no characteristic diffraction peak. The XRD spectral analysis shows that DPA-CuNCs have no crystallinity, and the results indicate the successful synthesis of DPA-CuNCs [35]. There are two distinct peaks at 934.6 eV and 955.2 eV in the XPS energy spectrum of 2p of Cu (Fig. 2(b)). The strong peak is 2p3/2 of Cu, and the gentle rise is 2p1/2; the 2p3/2 peak position represents zero-valent Cu, and the 2p1/2 peak position represents monovalent Cu. It can be concluded from the XPS plots that the valence of Cu in DPA-CuNCs is zero-valent Cu and monovalent Cu. At the same time, the absence of the characteristic peak for Cu(II) around 942.0 eV indicates the lack of Cu(II) in DPA-CuNCs, further demonstrating the successful synthesis of DPA-CuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites
ce:section.ce:para: To study the structure and elemental composition of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites, we will analyze them using the following tests: The infrared spectra of DPA-CuNCs, CQDs@SiO2, and CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites were obtained by Fourier infrared spectrometer testing, as shown in Fig.S1. The FT-IR spectrum shows a vast region at 3460 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H, and the infrared spectral band observed at 1090 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the Si-O-C group. In the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs spectrogram, the characteristic peak of the CO stretch mode appears at 1780 cm−1 and has the most vigorous intensity. Near 1635 cm−1, the infrared spectrum shows an absorption band corresponding to the N-H bending, indicating that penicillamine is immobilized on the cluster surface by Cu-S bonds. The S-H stretching pattern disappeared at 2511 cm−1. The absorption peaks of 1153 cm−1 and 950 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of Si-O-Si and Si-O-H bonds, respectively, confirming that the hydrolysis and condensation of APTES formed a cross-linked organosilicon network [36,37].

EDS tested the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite. The element and content analysis can be carried out according to the different X-ray photon characteristics of various elements in the sample. As shown in Fig. S2, elements C, O, S, N, and Cu exist in the composite, and SiO2 nanospheres are synthesized. The mass fraction ratio of each component is 39.5%, 29.7%, 26.7%, 3.7%, and 0.2%, and the synthesized copper clusters were doped with silica microspheres [38,39], which proved that the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanocomposite was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on fluorescence properties of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the optical properties of the prepared CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites, fluorescence tests were performed using fluorescence spectrometers and UV-Vis spectrometers. Firstly, the optical properties of the samples were studied. The emission peak of DPA-CuNCs is at 650 nm. From Fig. 3a. The fluorescence color of DPA-CuNCs is orange-red under UV light (365 nm) and milky white under sunlight (Fig. 3a inset). Fig. 3b shows that the peak position of DPA-CuNCs does not change with the increase of excitation wavelength, indicating that DPA-CuNCs do not have fluorescence dependence. From Fig. 3c, it is found that CQDs@SiO2 has a strong emission peak at 470 nm, and DPA-CuNCs simultaneously have a prominent excitation peak at 350 nm, which is also consistent with 350 nm in the UV-Vis spectrogram (Fig. 3c). The CQDs@SiO2 fluorescence color is blue when exposed to UV light and colorless in sunlight (Fig. 3c inset). Similarly, the CQDs@SiO2 peak position did not change when the excitation wavelength gradually increased, indicating that CQDs@SiO2 has no fluorescence dependence (Fig. 3d). It can be found from the figure that the excitation wavelength of DPA-CuNCs is 350 nm and the emission wavelength is 650 nm, while CQDs@SiO2 has a strong emission peak at 470 nm, while CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs has a strong emission peak at 650 nm and 450 nm, respectively. During the self-assembly of DPA-CuNCs and CQDs, there is almost no overlap between the fluorescence excitation band of DPA-CuNCs and the emission band of CQDs, indicating that the emission peaks of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs are the respective emission peaks of DPA-CuNCs, CQDs@SiO2.[40,41].

When the excitation wavelength is 350 nm, the optimal emission wavelengths of DPA-CuNCs and CQDs@SiO2 double fluorescence emission composites are 650 and 450 nm, respectively ( Fig. 4). When the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite is excited at 350 nm, emission peaks appear at 650 and 450 nm, respectively. As can be seen in the inset of Fig. 4b, under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp: CQDs@SiO2 fluoresces blue, DPA-CuNCs fluoresces orange-red at 650 nm, and CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs fluoresces purplish-red, which further proves that the double fluorescence emission composite (CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs) was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the sensitivity of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs fluorescent composites for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: An excellent fluorescent nanocomposite material has not only high sensitivity but also high accuracy. We selected a variety of standard metal ions, including Ag+, Ca2+, Cr6+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mn7+, Mg2+, Na+, Co2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, and Ni2+, and added them to CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanocomposite solution respectively under the same experimental conditions. To evaluate the specificity of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanocomposite, and finally, add a particular concentration of Hg2+ to measure the anti-interference ability of the sensor. As shown in Fig. 5a, in the presence of other metal ions, the ratio of I650/I450 did not change significantly compared with that before, indicating that CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs fluorescent nanomaterials are insensitive to these metal ions and have much better specificity for Hg2+ than other metal ions. Suppose Hg2+ is added to CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanocomposite solution. In that case, the ratio of I650/I450 is significantly reduced, indicating that the interference of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs fluorescent nanomaterials on Hg2+ detection can be ignored in the presence of other metal ions [42–44]. It is further demonstrated that the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite has high specificity for Hg2+. The fluorescence pattern is shown in Fig. 5b.

The fluorescence response to Hg2+ was different between CQDs@SiO2 and DPA-CuNCs with different characteristic peak positions ( Fig. 6). At the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the emission intensity of orange-red fluorescence of DPA-CuNCs at 650 nm gradually decreased after adding different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.06–80 μM). In comparison, the emission peak at CQDs@SiO2 at 470 nm remained unchanged. In other words, the orange-red fluorescence of DPA-CuNCs was quenched with increasing Hg2+ concentration, while the CQDs@SiO2 blue light fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged. The fluorescence color of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite changed from fuchsia to blue. Hg2+ quenching of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites It is possible that a chemical reaction occurred between the surface groups of DPA-CuNCs and Hg2+, leading to the quenching of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites [43,45].

As shown in Fig. 7, with the increase of Hg2+ concentration (0.06–100 μM), the quenching effect of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs fluorescence composite becomes more and more apparent. When the concentration of Hg2+ reaches 100 μM, the fluorescence of DPA-CuNCs is wholly quenched. The blue fluorescence intensity of CQDs@SiO2 is stable and almost remains unchanged, indicating that Hg2+ has virtually no effect on the emission intensity of CQDs@SiO2. However, the fluorescence intensity of DPA-CuNCs decreased significantly. Thus it can be concluded that the quenching of Hg2+ by CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs fluorescent composites may be caused by the chemical reaction between the surface groups of DPA-CuNCs and Hg2+, resulting in the quenching of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs fluorescent composites. This is the advantage of the dual emission ratio fluorescence sensor; establishing an internal standard significantly reduces the interference of many difficult-to-control factors so that the detection results are more accurate and the response range is more comprehensive. In addition, the minimum detection limit of Hg2+ of the prepared CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanomaterials was found to be 1 μM.

Fig. 8 shows the scatter plot of the fluorescence intensity ratio at I650/I450 of the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite material and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0–100 μM. From the relationship between I650/I450 fluorescence intensity ratio and the Hg2+ concentration, it can be seen that I650/I450 fluorescence intensity ratio decreased significantly with the increase in Hg2+ concentration. In addition, as shown in the inset of Fig. 8, when the concentration of Hg2+ is in the range of 0–5 μM, there is a good linear relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 650 nm and 450 nm of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite and the concentration of Hg2+ (0–5 μM). The linear regression equation is Y = −0.0684x+ 0.7995, R2 = 0.997, and the lowest detection limit (LOD) of Hg2+ response is 1 μM. Therefore, the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite has specificity for Hg2+ detection in the water environment. In addition, it has high detection sensitivity at low Hg2+ concentration.

When the fluorescence of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite is quenched by Hg2+, with the gradual increase of Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–80 µM, under sunlight, the solution color is transparent. It does not change significantly, but under ultraviolet light, The fluorescence of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite changed from fuchsia to blue, as shown in Fig. 9. This indicates that the ratio of emission peak intensity at 650 nm and 450 nm of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite can lead to the change of fluorescence emission color of this dual fluorescence emission composite to realize the detection of Hg2+ [46].

Fig. 10 shows the CIE (CIE 1931 chromaticity) diagram when Hg2+ quenches the fluorescence of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanocomposite. The color changes of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs nanocomposites with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–80 μM under 365 nm UV light are consistent with Fig. 10. As the concentration of Hg2+ (0.06–100 μM) increased, the fluorescence of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite changed from magenta to blue, and the magenta fluorescence was quenched to realize the specific detection of Hg2+ [47].

In order to verify the effective detection performance of the synthesized CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites, we investigated the stability of the nanocomposites under different conditions. Fig. S3a shows the effect of pH on the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites. The intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks located at 450 nm and 650 nm changed continuously with the pH value in an acidic environment at pH 1–5. The I650/I450 fluorescence ratio of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission did not change regularly. However, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites showed a slow and regular decrease when the pH value was varied from 6 to 14. The results show that the dual fluorescence-emitting composites have a stable fluorescence intensity ratio at 650 nm and 450 nm in different alkaline environments, indicating that the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence-emitting composites have specific stability in alkaline environments. Fig. S3b shows the fluorescence spectra of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emitting composites with different pH values [48]. Also, to investigate the effect of temperature variation on the composite, we tested the nanofluorescence intensity at different temperatures. The experimental temperature interval was 10–80 °C. The test results are shown in Fig. S4a. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence-emitting composites was almost constant as the temperature slowly increased from 10° to 50°C. When the temperature gradually increased from 50° to 80°C, the fluorescence emission peak at 450 nm hardly changed, and the fluorescence emission peak at 650 nm gradually decreased. CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites showed a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. The results indicate that the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites have a stable fluorescence intensity ratio when the temperature is in the interval of 10 degrees to 50 degrees, which has a specific stability in this temperature interval. Fig.S4b shows the fluorescence spectra of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emitting composites at different temperatures. In summary, CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites are suitable for the detection of Hg2+ in water samples in alkaline environments in the temperature range of 10–50 °C and pH 6–14.

In Fig. S5a, the fluorescence stability line diagram of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites in different concentrations of NaCl (0.002–1.7 mm) was studied in this experiment, and the results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites remained almost unchanged. This means that the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite is stable in saline solution. Fig. S5b Fluorescence emission spectra of double fluorescence emission composites at NaCl concentrations ranging from 0.002 to 1.7 mm. The results show that the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite has good salt tolerance and can be used under different salt solution concentration conditions, significantly broadening the application environment [48].

The CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite also has excellent storage stability and can maintain bright and stable fluorescence in the surrounding environment for more than 30 days without any protection. Fig. S6a shows the results of the composite placed at room temperature for 30 days, and the fluorescence ratio does not change, indicating that the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite can be stored for a long time, simplifying the detection process and ensure the stability of detection. The fluorescence spectrogram of the effect of placement time on the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite is shown in Fig. S6a. The effect of UV irradiation time on CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite was demonstrated. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite remained stable when the UV irradiation time gradually increased within 40 min. It is proved that the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite has high photochemical stability, which indicates that it can be stored for a long time and can still ensure the accuracy of detection results under different illumination times. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites affected by light duration are shown in Fig. S6b.

In order to verify the application value of prepared CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite in actual water samples, the method of spiked recovery was adopted in this work to demonstrate the detection effect of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite on Hg2+ in actual water samples. Tap water and Changchun NanHu water were selected as actual water samples for detection and were filtered by a 0.22 μM membrane before use without any other treatment. 5.00 μM, 50.00 μM, and 100.00 μM Hg2+ were added to tap water and lake water. The detection results are shown in Table 1. Although there is some interference, the actual detection results are satisfactory, and the recovery rate is reasonable. According to Table 1, the estimated recovery rate of actual water samples is between 93.40%− 106.40%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is between 1.6%− 5.4% (n = 3). Three essays obtained this under the same experimental conditions. The results show that the CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite is suitable for detecting Hg2+ in actual water.

We have successfully designed and fabricated a novel CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composite. In the preparation process, ethyl orthosilicate was selected as the silicon source, and CQDs were synthesized by microwave method using citric acid as carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source. The prepared CQDs@SiO2 and DPA-CuNCs were combined by magnetic stirring to form CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composites. The resulting CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual fluorescence emission composites exhibit two fluorescence emission peaks of different wavelengths when excited at a wavelength of 350 nm and have a high fluorescence quenching effect in the presence of Hg2+. During detection, the orange-red fluorescence of DPA-CuNCs could be quenched by Hg2+, while the blue emission of CQDs@SiO2 remained stable. At the same time, the fluorescence color of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs double fluorescence emission composite changed from purplish red to blue, thus realizing the fluorescence detection of Hg2+. The detection limit of CQDs@SiO2/CuNCs dual-emission fluorescence nanocomposite can be as low as 1 μM, and the novel dual-emission fluorescence nanocomposite has been successfully used to detect Hg2+ in water samples from Nan Hu Lake in Changchun, which has important value in the detection of heavy metal ions in the water environment.
The first two authors of Mao Wang and Hefei Huang contributed equally to this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 52273122), the Program of Jilin Provincial Department of Science & Technology (Grant No. 20220203060SF) and the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University (sklssm2023030).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.132080.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mao Wang is currently a master student under the supervision of associate professor Xudong Yang in the School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology. His research interest is synthesis and characterization of fluorescent nanomaterials.

Hefei Huang is currently a Undergraduates under the supervision of associate professor Xudong Yang in the School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology. His research interest is synthesis and characterization of fluorescent nanomaterials.

Lei Wang is currently a master student under the supervision of associate professor Xudong Yang in the School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology. Her research interest is environmental functional nanocomposites.

Menglei Sun is currently a master student under the supervision of associate professor Xudong Yang in the School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology. Her research interest is synthesis and characterization of fluorescent nanomaterials.

Haoting Hou is currently a master student under the supervision of associate professor Xudong Yang in the School of Chemical Engineering, Changchun University of Technology. Her research interest is synthesis and characterization of fluorescent nanomaterials.

Xudong Yang is associate professor of Materials Chemistry in the School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology. He received his Ph.D. degree in the major of polymer chemistry and physics from State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University in 2014. His research interests include the development of inorganic semiconductor nanostructures and their applications in biomedical and optoelectronic.
